Exploratory within-sample evidence from the state trust subgroup (Appendix I) is consistent with this mechanism: even among Swedish respondents with the lowest trust in the state as a biodiversity finance administrator, the top-ranked instrument is mandatory corporate offsetting, not voluntary donation. Institutional distrust appears to redirect mandatory preferences toward corporate liability rather than producing voluntary instrument preference — suggesting the theorized inversion, if it occurs cross-nationally, may be specific to contexts where both state capacity and private corporate accountability are weak, not simply wherever state trust is low.

Crucially, we argue this inversion represents a *constrained, defensive* preference born of institutional failure rather than an endorsement of voluntary governance's structural efficacy. Bypassing a distrusted state via private donations or certified market offsets is a tactical choice to preserve marginal utility, not a reflection of an ideal policy architecture. A low-trust citizen who prefers voluntary instruments is not expressing that voluntary governance is better at solving collective action problems; they are expressing that they do not trust the state to implement mandatory mechanisms fairly.

This distinction carries direct implications for global biodiversity governance. The pervasive shift toward voluntary, market-based mechanisms under the GBF is frequently justified as matching decentralised citizen preferences. Our findings challenge this narrative: citizens in the context most structurally favorable to voluntary instrument support consistently prefer the re-centering of public authority. Voluntary instruments appear, in this light, as a second-best patch, and devolving governance to voluntary architectures may be better understood as institutional resignation than as progressive policy design. The Brazilian Cerrado offers a landscape-scale illustration: voluntary soy moratoria that achieved partial effectiveness in the Amazon were not extended to the Cerrado, where weaker regulatory infrastructure and monitoring contributed to continued biodiversity loss — a case in which the absence of a mandatory backstop proved decisive [@BrandaoCardosoGarrett2025]. Wherever state capacity is sufficient, relying on voluntary frameworks is likely misaligned with citizen preferences; in lower-capacity contexts, the priority should be building the institutional infrastructure that makes mandatory mechanisms legitimate and effective, rather than cementing voluntary markets as a permanent substitute.

## Limitations

Several limitations should be noted. First, the analysis sample is large but was recruited via an online panel, which may oversample individuals with higher digital literacy and environmental awareness, which likely contributes to the high giving rate (83.3%) relative to laboratory donation experiment benchmarks. Second, voluntary preference scores are computed only from mixed-instrument tasks and exclude opt-out choices, which may introduce selection. Third, the preregistration covers the CL and MXL models but not the donation-based heterogeneity analysis; the latter should be interpreted as exploratory and hypothesis-generating rather than confirmatory. Fourth, the instrument coding as voluntary vs. mandatory is a simplification: certification can be seen as semi-mandatory if it becomes the de facto market standard, and offsetting markets vary in their voluntary vs. regulated character.

Fifth, the survey presents the DCE before the donation experiment. Completing the DCE — which repeatedly frames biodiversity conservation as a policy priority and makes financing mechanisms salient — may have primed respondents to donate more generously than they would in the absence of the DCE framing. If the priming effect were heterogeneous, this would create an endogeneity problem: $G_i$ would be a function of the DCE treatment itself, biasing the VP regression. We conduct an exploratory check by regressing donation amount on the VP score (number of voluntary DCE choices relative to mixed tasks), conditional on environmental concern and all covariates. If DCE exposure primed more voluntary-minded respondents to donate more, the VP coefficient should be positive. Instead, the coefficient is negative and statistically significant ($\hat\gamma = -94$ SEK, SE = 24, $p < 0.001$): respondents who chose voluntary options more often in the DCE donated *less* in the donation experiment, conditional on environmental concern and other covariates. This is the mirror image of the forward regression (higher donors prefer mandatory instruments) and argues against positive primin. The DCE did not cause voluntary-minded respondents to donate more. The mean donation rate (83.3%) may still be elevated relative to a no-DCE baseline, but this level effect would shift the intercept of the donation distribution upward equally for all respondents; it would not alter the gradient between higher and lower donors that drives the VP regression coefficient. The slope-based inference on which the strategic preference test rests is therefore structurally robust to order-induced priming of the donation level. We cannot rule out more subtle forms of priming, and future studies should randomise the order of DCE and donation task between subjects to test this mechanism directly.

A further methodological caveat is that the donation experiment and DCE measure different objects: personal charitable giving versus preferences over societal policy instruments. The regression uses the former as a behavioral proxy for the disposition that generates the latter, which assumes these two decisions share a common underlying motivation. This is a plausible but untestable link without real-world policy outcome data.

Sixth, this paper examines how financing instrument design influences willingness to pay, not the effectiveness of the instruments themselves. Given that respondents most strongly prefer biodiversity offsetting, it is worth noting that actually existing offsetting schemes have faced considerable challenges [@Maron2025; @Wunder2025]. Existing programmes have shown that restoring seemingly equivalent biodiversity via compensation is difficult, not all offsetting markets comply strictly with the mitigation hierarchy, and inflated baselines can lead to an overproduction of offsets without material impact [@Bigger2021]. The most preferred instrument may not be the most effective, at least not without robust governance and political will.

Finally, this DCE design was deliberately narrowed to address respondents as individual voters or consumers, which made the experiment operational. While biodiversity offsetting primarily places obligations on developers, it was studied here as an instrument whose costs would ultimately fall on consumers. Our design thereby leaves open the question of whether alternative fiscal or regulatory instruments, targeting firms or industries directly, would receive different levels of support.

# Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

The dominant paradigm of global environmental governance has, for three decades, bet heavily on voluntary instruments, specifically private donations, market certification, corporate offsetting, and non-state initiatives, to fill the gap left by gridlocked multilateral treaties. This bet rests on a behavioral assumption: that environmental concern, once activated, channels naturally into voluntary action at scale. We have tested that assumption at its own best case.

In Sweden, where social trust, environmental identity, and per-capita conservation spending rank among the world's highest, citizens consistently prefer mandatory biodiversity financing instruments over voluntary alternatives. Corporate offsetting and tax payment are preferred over donation and certification across all pre-registered model specifications. Public monitoring commands a WTP of approximately SEK 1,600 per year, suggesting that citizens place high value on state oversight and verification — a direct economic expression of regulatory legitimacy.

The preference structure also reveals a meaningful within-mandatory distinction. Each instrument in our DCE is a bundle of attributes: source type (who pays and under what obligation), land governance, monitoring, and price are varied orthogonally. The source attribute captures two separate dimensions simultaneously: whether compliance is legally required (mandatory tax and offsetting versus voluntary donation and certification) and who bears the primary obligation (households via tax versus corporations via offsetting). The offsetting premium relative to tax — the highest-ranked source attribute in both CL and MXL — suggests that beyond preferring mandatory instruments in general, Swedish citizens specifically prefer placing the compliance burden on corporate actors rather than households. This is consistent with a "polluter pays" norm and with the evidence that endorsers of offsetting specifically trust private markets to deliver environmental outcomes. The mandate to fund biodiversity is not merely "state vs. market" or "public vs. private" — it is also about which institutional actor should carry primary legal responsibility.

Critically, the behavioral logic underlying voluntary governance is rejected even in this most-favorable context. Environmentally committed and personally generous respondents prefer mandatory instruments, not because they want to free-ride on voluntary schemes, but because they recognise that voluntary participation cannot guarantee adequate provision at scale. The most motivated actors demand binding collective mechanisms, the assurance that voluntary instruments cannot provide.

We theorize that voluntary instrument preference, where it appears globally, reflects a constrained, defensive choice born of institutional weakness rather than an unconstrained normative endorsement of voluntary governance. Citizens bypass distrusted states via private channels not because private channels are better, but because mandatory mechanisms are unavailable or untrustworthy in their context. The implication for GBF implementation is direct: in high-capacity state contexts, voluntary frameworks are structurally misaligned with citizen preferences; in lower-capacity contexts, the long-run priority is building the institutional infrastructure to make mandatory mechanisms legitimate.

Future research should replicate these findings in lower social trust contexts to test the predicted preference inversion, and should pre-register the interaction hypothesis to enable confirmatory testing with adequate statistical power in the key subgroup cells. If mandatory preference persists across institutional contexts, the case for re-centering biodiversity finance architecture around binding public mechanisms becomes considerably stronger.

# Author Contributions {.unnumbered}

 **Nils Droste**: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Writing — original draft, Writing — review and editing. **Jens Christiansen**: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Writing — review and editing. **Yohei Mitani**: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing — review and editing.

# Declaration of Generative AI and AI-Assisted Technologies in the Manuscript Preparation Process {.unnumbered}

During the preparation of this work the authors used Claude (Anthropic) to assist with manuscript drafting and revision, literature identification, and R code development for statistical analyses. After using this tool, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the published article.

# Data Availability {.unnumbered}

The survey was conducted under ethics approval from Etikprövningsmyndigheten (ID 2025-04420-01). Participants provided informed consent to online publication of anonymized data. The anonymized survey dataset, analysis code, and DCE design matrix will be deposited in a public repository upon acceptance; the repository DOI will be provided at that stage. Free-text responses are excluded from the public deposit to preclude re-identification.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

# Appendix {.unnumbered}

## A. Survey instrument overview {.unnumbered}

### A.1 Financing instrument descriptions shown to respondents {.unnumbered}

Prior to the DCE tasks, respondents read the following definitions (shown in Swedish on screen):

> **Tax payment:** An increase in your net tax contribution, with an explicit purpose of supporting biodiversity conservation and restoration.

> **Voluntary donation:** A voluntary private donation to civil society organisations that work with restoring and conserving biodiversity.

> **Market certification:** A higher price for a certified commodity, which is produced in a biodiversity-friendly way.

> **Mandatory offsetting:** The state requires companies to offset their biodiversity impact and market prices would increase as a consequence.

Following these definitions, respondents completed matching exercises (Q1) linking real-world examples to each instrument type, ensuring that DCE preferences were elicited from respondents who had actively processed the instrument concepts. The definitions above were also accessible during the DCE tasks.

### A.2 Land ownership and monitoring descriptions {.unnumbered}

> **Public lands:** Biodiversity protection on publicly owned land by creating protected areas.

> **Industrial scale corporate owner:** Biodiversity protection on large scale industrial land by paying the landowner for setting aside land from production.

> **Small scale private owner:** Biodiversity protection on small scale private land by paying the landowner for setting aside land for biodiversity.

> **Public administration (monitoring):** Public monitoring and enforcement by a state authority or agency.

> **Private, independent audit (monitoring):** Monitoring by the private landowner and a third-party auditor, such as a consultancy.

## B. DCE design matrix {.unnumbered}

[TABLE: Attribute levels by block and task number; full design matrix available in `design/Trial 3 - factorial grouped, svensk.xlsx`]

## C. Preregistration details {.unnumbered}

This study was pre-registered at AsPredicted prior to data collection:

- Original registration: #273748, February 15, 2026 (available at aspredicted.org/im78mi.pdf)
- Updated registration: #287153, April 23, 2026, adding response quality exclusion criteria (available at aspredicted.org/zs4y9e.pdf)

Pre-registered analyses: conditional logit and mixed logit models (Sections 4.1 and 5.1–5.2). All other analyses in this paper are exploratory and were not preregistered.

## D. Robustness checks and power analysis {.unnumbered}

**OLS results (primary specification in fractional logit; OLS for comparison).** The OLS models M1–M4 described in the pre-revision manuscript are retained here for comparison. The average partial effects (APEs) of the fractional logit models in @tbl-reg and the OLS coefficients are nearly identical (within rounding), confirming that the bounded distribution of VP does not substantially distort the OLS estimates in this application.

**Sensitivity: n_mixed ≥ 3.** Rerunning the primary fractional logit model (FL3) on the subsample of respondents with at least 3 informative mixed tasks ($n = 1{,}478$, dropping 385 respondents with fewer than 3 mixed tasks) yields coefficient estimates consistent with the full sample: donation effect $\hat\beta_1 = -0.102$ (SE = 0.035, $p = 0.003$), environmental concern $\hat\beta_2 = -0.218$ (SE = 0.042, $p < 0.001$), interaction $\hat\beta_3 = -0.033$ (SE = 0.037, $p = 0.376$). The interaction remains non-significant and the main effects strengthen, confirming that the additive pattern is not an artefact of measurement noise.

**Power analysis.** Under the OLS model for VP with $n = 1{,}863$, $\overline{\text{VP}} = 0.431$, and $\text{SD}(\text{VP}) = 0.320$ (and standardised predictors), the minimum detectable effect size for the interaction at 80% power ($\alpha = 0.05$, two-sided) is $|\hat\beta_3| \approx 0.021$. The observed interaction estimate ($\hat\beta_3 = -0.002$) is an order of magnitude below this threshold, indicating the study had adequate power to detect medium-sized interactions but found no evidence of even a small effect. The null interaction result is therefore informative: we can reasonably rule out interactions of the magnitude originally hypothesised.

## E. Subgroup heterogeneity table {.unnumbered}

```{r}
#| label: tbl-het
#| tbl-cap: "Pre-registered: CL instrument coefficients by subgroup (vs. tax baseline)"
#| eval: true

het_tbl <- tibble(
  Subgroup = c(
    "Full sample",
    "Female", "Male",
    "Income: low", "Income: mid", "Income: high",
    "Low env. att.", "High env. att."
  ),
  N = c(
    "16,808", "8,208", "8,600",
    "2,768", "10,720", "3,320",
    "6,560", "10,248"
  ),
  Don = c(
    "-0.376*** (0.032)",
    "-0.365*** (0.046)", "-0.388*** (0.045)",
    "-0.252**  (0.080)", "-0.386*** (0.040)", "-0.443*** (0.071)",
    "+0.053    (0.052)", "-0.659*** (0.042)"
  ),
  DonWTP = c(
    "-1,929",
    "-1,851", "-2,009",
    "-1,383", "-1,867", "-2,645",
    "+240", "-3,572"
  ),
  Cert = c(
    "-0.082*  (0.032)",
    "-0.096*  (0.046)", "-0.069   (0.045)",
    "+0.044   (0.080)", "-0.096*  (0.040)", "-0.135†  (0.071)",
    "+0.169** (0.052)", "-0.242*** (0.041)"
  ),
  Off = c(
    "+0.146*** (0.034)",
    "+0.146**  (0.049)", "+0.146**  (0.048)",
    "+0.118    (0.084)", "+0.167*** (0.043)", "+0.095    (0.077)",
    "+0.223*** (0.056)", "+0.089*   (0.044)"
  )
)

het_tbl |>
  kableExtra::kbl(booktabs = TRUE, escape = TRUE, longtable = FALSE,
                  col.names = c("Subgroup", "N obs", "Donation (SE)",
                                "WTP SEK/yr", "Certification (SE)",
                                "Offsetting (SE)"),
                  align = c("l","r","r","r","r","r")) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position"),
                            bootstrap_options = c("condensed"),
                            font_size = 9, full_width = TRUE) |>
  kableExtra::pack_rows("Gender", 2, 3) |>
  kableExtra::pack_rows("Income", 4, 6) |>
  kableExtra::pack_rows("Environmental attitude", 7, 8) |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = c(
      "Separate CL models per subgroup; instrument coefficients vs. tax baseline.",
      "WTP = coef / |price coef| * 1000 SEK/year. HC3 SEs in parentheses.",
      "Income: low = 0--20k SEK/month; mid = 20--100k; high = >100k (cats 3--5).",
      "Env. att.: median split on Q11 (Environmental importance, 1-5).",
      "† p<0.1; * p<0.05; ** p<0.01; *** p<0.001."
    ),
    general_title = "Note:", footnote_as_chunk = FALSE, escape = TRUE
  )
```

## F. Theoretical framework: strategic hypothesis {.unnumbered}

We sketch the strategic voluntary preference hypothesis in a simple expected-utility framework to clarify what the fractional logit interaction test identifies.

Let respondent $i$ hold environmental valuation $b_i \geq 0$ and private willingness to contribute $c_i \geq 0$, revealed by the dictator-game donation $G_i$. Consider a DCE choice between two instruments:

- **Mandatory** ($m$): achieves the conservation target with certainty; respondent pays a uniform cost $\tau$.
- **Voluntary** ($v$): achieves fractional provision $Q_v \in (0,1)$ (the target is underprovided because participation is voluntary); the free-riding respondent pays nothing.

Utility comparisons give a preference for voluntary iff $b_i Q_v > b_i - \tau$, i.e., $\tau > b_i(1 - Q_v)$.

The **strategic free-rider** has high $b_i$ but $G_i = 0$ (not willing to contribute personally, as revealed by the dictator game). If they believe others will contribute enough that $Q_v$ is non-negligible, they may prefer voluntary in the DCE while free-riding in practice — especially when $\tau$ is large. This predicts that among non-donors, higher environmental concern $b_i$ produces stronger voluntary preference. For donors ($G_i > 0$), the free-rider advantage disappears because they pay under voluntary as well, making the direction ambiguous.

The strategic hypothesis therefore predicts a positive interaction: $\beta_3 > 0$ in

$$\text{VP}_i = \alpha + \beta_1 G_i + \beta_2 B_i + \beta_3 (G_i \times B_i) + \mathbf{X}_i'\boldsymbol{\gamma} + \varepsilon_i,$$

where $G_i$ is the observed donation (proxy for $c_i$) and $B_i$ is stated environmental concern (proxy for $b_i$).

The **public-goods consequentialist** mechanism predicts $\beta_1 < 0$, $\beta_2 < 0$, $\beta_3 \approx 0$: both donation and concern independently shift preferences toward mandatory instruments, because more engaged respondents understand that voluntary provision will underperform. This predicts two additive main effects with no interaction.

We find APE$_1 = -0.030$ ($p < 0.001$), APE$_2 = -0.042$ ($p < 0.001$), APE$_3 = -0.002$ ($p = 0.792$) — consistent with the consequentialist mechanism and inconsistent with strategic free-riding.

## G. WTP-based robustness check {.unnumbered}

As an alternative outcome measure, we replace the VP score with individual-level WTP for donation relative to tax ($\widehat{\text{WTP}}_{\text{don},i}$, SEK/year), estimated by importance-sampling conditional means from the fitted MXL. For each respondent, we draw $S = 1{,}000$ parameter vectors from the population distribution $\mathcal{N}(\hat\mu, \hat\sigma^2)$, weight each draw by the respondent's likelihood of observed choices across all eight tasks, and take the importance-weighted mean as the individual posterior mean coefficient. WTP is then $\widehat{\text{WTP}}_{\text{don},i} = -\hat\beta_{\text{don},i} / (\hat\beta_{\text{price}}/1{,}000)$. Negative values indicate preference for mandatory instruments (tax) over donation.

Individual WTP estimates have mean $-$SEK 2,145/year (SD = 3,040; median $-$SEK 2,216), and correlate with the VP score at $r = 0.531$ across the same 1,863 respondents, confirming the two measures capture the same underlying preference dimension.

Stage-2 OLS regressing $\widehat{\text{WTP}}_{\text{don},i}$ on the same covariates as the VP model (HC3 SEs) yields: donation APE $\approx -$SEK 120 ($p = 0.122$), environmental concern $\approx -$SEK 312 ($p = 0.001$), interaction $\approx -$SEK 68 ($p = 0.364$). The environmental concern effect replicates strongly; the donation effect is directionally consistent but falls below conventional significance, reflecting the substantially higher variance of individual WTP estimates relative to VP scores. The null interaction replicates. These results support the VP-based findings for the env_z dimension and are consistent with the null on strategic behaviour, while suggesting that the donation signal, though robust under VP, may be weaker than it appears — an important caveat for future work using this two-stage design.

## H. Latent class model: convergent validity {.unnumbered}

As a further convergent validity check, we estimate a 2-class latent class multinomial logit (LC-MNL) on the DCE data using Apollo [@Hess2021]. LC models do not impose parametric assumptions on the distribution of unobserved heterogeneity (as the MXL does) and instead recover discrete latent segments with distinct preference vectors. If the data contain a mandatory-preference segment and a voluntary-preference segment, the LC model should recover them, and the segment membership pattern should align with the VP regression findings: the mandatory-preference class should be characterised by higher environmental concern and higher dictator game donations.

**Specification.** We estimate an unconditional 2-class LC-MNL with the same within-class utility specification as the CL (six attribute dummies plus price, plus an ASC for the opt-out). Class membership is unconditional at the estimation stage (a single intercept $\delta_0$ determining the log-odds of class membership). After estimation, we characterise the two classes by (i) their within-class utility vectors and (ii) the mean env\_z and don\_z of respondents assigned to each class by their posterior modal class membership probability. Estimation uses the BGW optimizer with starting values informed by the CL estimates; the optimizer converged (code 4, relative function convergence). Because LC log-likelihoods can have multiple local maxima, the single-start result should be treated cautiously; however, the qualitative conclusion — that no voluntary-preference segment is recovered — is robust to the starting values used (CL-informed starts covering both mandatory and voluntary preference patterns in each class). Robust sandwich SEs are reported to guard against numerical ill-conditioning of the Hessian. The analysis code is in `analysis/lcm_analysis.R`.

The utility specification for alternative $j \in \{a, b, \text{neither}\}$ in class $k$ is:

$$V_{ijt}^{(k)} = \beta_{\text{don}}^{(k)} D_{jt} + \beta_{\text{cert}}^{(k)} C_{jt} + \beta_{\text{off}}^{(k)} O_{jt} + \beta_{\text{ind}}^{(k)} I_{jt} + \beta_{\text{pland}}^{(k)} L_{jt} + \beta_{\text{pmon}}^{(k)} M_{jt} + \beta_{\text{price}}^{(k)} p_{jt} + \text{ASC}^{(k)} \cdot \mathbf{1}[j=\text{neither}]$$

with class membership probability $\pi_1 = 1/(1+e^{\delta_0})$ and $\pi_2 = e^{\delta_0}/(1+e^{\delta_0})$.

**Results.** Table H.1 reports estimated within-class utilities (robust standard errors), class shares, and WTP. The model fit improves substantially over the CL (LL = $-16{,}647.5$ vs CL LL = $-16{,}790.8$; 16 additional parameters).

```{r}
#| label: tbl-lcm
#| tbl-cap: "2-class LC-MNL: within-class coefficients (robust SE), WTP, and class characterisation"
#| eval: true

lcm_tbl <- tibble(
  Parameter = c(
    "ASC (neither/opt-out)",
    "Donation (vs. tax)",
    "Certification (vs. tax)",
    "Offsetting (vs. tax)",
    "Industrial land (vs. small-scale)",
    "Public land (vs. small-scale)",
    "Public monitoring (vs. private audit)",
    "Annual cost (kSEK/year)",
    "",
    "Log-likelihood",
    "Class share",
    "N assigned (modal)",
    "Mean env\\_z (posterior)",
    "Mean don\\_z (posterior)"
  ),
  C1_coef = c(
    "-9.814*  (5.039)",
    "-1.682*** (0.514)",
    "-0.212   (0.539)",
    "+0.333   (0.332)",
    "-0.535** (0.222)",
    "+0.672*** (0.208)",
    "+1.017*** (0.176)",
    "-0.958*** (0.134)",
    "",
    "-16,647.5",
    "30.5\\%",
    "638",
    "+0.094",
    "+0.033"
  ),
  C1_wtp = c(
    "---",
    "-1,756",
    "-222",
    "+347",
    "-558",
    "+702",
    "+1,062",
    "---",
    "", "", "", "", "", ""
  ),
  C2_coef = c(
    "-0.320*** (0.098)",
    "-0.174*   (0.088)",
    "-0.117   (0.102)",
    "+0.086†  (0.066)",
    "+0.123*   (0.056)",
    "+0.161*** (0.052)",
    "+0.283*** (0.040)",
    "-0.092*** (0.025)",
    "",
    "---",
    "69.5\\%",
    "1,463",
    "-0.041",
    "-0.015"
  ),
  C2_wtp = c(
    "---",
    "-1,897",
    "-1,277",
    "+941",
    "+1,344",
    "+1,754",
    "+3,089",
    "---",
    "", "", "", "", "", ""
  )
)

lcm_tbl |>
  kableExtra::kbl(booktabs = TRUE, escape = FALSE, longtable = FALSE,
                  col.names = c("Parameter",
                                "Coeff (Rob. SE)", "WTP SEK/yr",
                                "Coeff (Rob. SE)", "WTP SEK/yr"),
                  align = c("l","r","r","r","r")) |>
  kableExtra::add_header_above(c(" " = 1,
                                  "Class 1 — strong mandatory (30.5\\%)" = 2,
                                  "Class 2 — moderate mandatory (69.5\\%)" = 2),
                                escape = FALSE) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position"),
                            bootstrap_options = c("condensed"),
                            full_width = TRUE, font_size = 9) |>
  kableExtra::pack_rows("Model fit and class composition", 10, 14) |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = c(
      "Baseline: tax + small-scale private land + private audit.",
      "Robust SEs in parentheses. WTP in SEK/year (ratio estimator).",
      "Class share from unconditional membership (delta\\_0 = 0.826, SE = 0.046).",
      "Posterior env\\_z and don\\_z: mean standardised values for respondents",
      "  assigned to each class by modal posterior probability.",
      "Final LL reported for the combined model (both classes jointly).",
      "Estimated with BGW optimizer (Apollo); convergence code 4 (relative function convergence,",
      "  favorable). Hessian condition number = 3.54e-6; robust sandwich SEs used throughout.",
      "Single-start estimation from starting values informed by CL estimates.",
      "† p<0.1; * p<0.05; ** p<0.01; *** p<0.001."
    ),
    general_title = "Note:", footnote_as_chunk = FALSE, escape = TRUE
  )
```

**Interpretation.** The LC model does not recover a voluntary-preference segment. Both classes have negative WTP for donation-financed programmes relative to the tax baseline (Class 1: $-$SEK 1,756/year; Class 2: $-$SEK 1,897/year), and both assign positive WTP to offsetting. The heterogeneity the LC model captures is in the *intensity* of mandatory preference and in price sensitivity, not in its direction. Class 1 (30.5%) is characterised by a very negative opt-out ASC (near-certain choice of a programme option over the status quo), very high price sensitivity ($\hat\beta_{\text{price}} = -0.958$), and a strong negative utility for donation. Class 2 (69.5%) has more moderate parameters consistent with the CL mean estimates.

The posterior class characterisation provides weak convergent support for the VP regression: respondents assigned to the strongly mandatory-preferring Class 1 have marginally higher mean env\_z (+0.094) and don\_z (+0.033) than those in Class 2 ($-$0.041 and $-$0.015 respectively), consistent with the VP regression direction (higher env\_z and don\_z predict lower VP, i.e., stronger mandatory preference). The differences are small, however, indicating that the LC classes are defined primarily by preference intensity and price sensitivity rather than by environmental attitudes or donation behaviour.

Taken together, the LC results provide convergent validity to the primary CL and MXL findings: there is no latent segment of the Swedish population that prefers voluntary biodiversity financing instruments, and the aggregate mandatory preference is not an artefact of averaging over a voluntary-preference minority.

## I. Exploratory subgroup heterogeneity {.unnumbered}

*These analyses were not pre-registered and should be treated as exploratory.*

Figure I.1 extends the pre-registered subgroup analysis (Appendix E) to three further dimensions: party vote intention (q16), trust in the state as a biodiversity finance administrator (q2_1), and belief in Swedish policy consequentiality (q14, "Sweden will follow through on its biodiversity policy targets"). Subgroup conditional logit models follow the same specification as the main CL. Code is in `analysis/heterogeneity_exploratory.R`.

**Party vote (q16).** The political divide runs not between mandatory and voluntary instruments but between state-coercive and market/corporate accountability mechanisms. Left-bloc voters (S/V/MP, n = 796) strongly prefer the state tax option (donation: $\hat\beta = -0.779^{***}$; certification: $-0.362^{***}$; offsetting: $0.013$), rejecting both voluntary instruments outright. Centre-right voters (M/C/KD/L, n = 523) and Sweden Democrats (SD, n = 287) rank corporate offsetting highest (offsetting: $0.360^{***}$ and $0.262^{**}$, respectively), but also show a modest preference for market certification over state taxation (certification: $0.135^{*}$ and $0.162^{\dagger}$). Individual voluntary donation is the consistent outlier: strongly rejected by left-bloc voters ($-0.779^{***}$) and statistically indistinguishable from tax among centre-right and SD voters ($-0.079$ and $-0.059$, both $p > 0.5$).

The pattern qualifies the aggregate mandatory preference finding: what holds robustly across all political blocs is the rejection of individual voluntary donation, not a uniform preference for mandatory instruments over all voluntary alternatives. For right-leaning voters, market certification — a supply-chain accountability mechanism operating through price signals rather than state extraction — is preferred over the state tax instrument. The politically salient dimension is state authority versus market/corporate accountability, not mandatory versus voluntary governance per se.

**State trust (q2_1).** Respondents with high trust in the state strongly prefer tax (donation: $-0.760^{***}$; offsetting: $0.001$). Respondents with low state trust show a positive coefficient on offsetting ($0.299^{***}$) and marginal preference for certification and donation over tax — but offsetting, a mandatory corporate accountability mechanism, remains their top choice. Low state trust does not produce voluntary instrument preference; it shifts preference within mandatory instruments toward corporate liability and away from state extraction. This nuances the boundary-conditions argument: institutional distrust appears to redirect mandatory preferences rather than eliminate them.

**Policy consequentiality (q14).** Mandatory preference is completely stable across consequentiality beliefs. Respondents skeptical that Sweden will implement its biodiversity targets show strong aversion to donation ($-0.411^{***}$) and slight aversion to certification ($-0.147^{**}$), closely mirroring the high-consequentiality subgroup. The mandatory/voluntary distinction is not driven by optimism about Swedish policy delivery.

```{r}
#| label: fig-het-exp
#| fig-cap: "Exploratory subgroup heterogeneity: instrument coefficients relative to tax baseline (95% CI). Panel A: party vote intention (q16); Panel B: trust in state as biodiversity finance administrator (q2_1, tercile); Panel C: belief that Sweden will follow through on biodiversity policy targets (q14, median split). All analyses exploratory (not pre-registered)."
#| fig-width: 6.5
#| fig-height: 7.5
#| eval: true

all_exp <- readRDS(here::here("analysis", "heterogeneity_exploratory.rds"))

subgroup_order <- rev(c(
  "Left (S/V/MP)", "Centre-right", "SD", "No pref / DK",
  "High state trust", "Mid state trust", "Low state trust",
  "High consequentiality", "Low consequentiality"
))

instr_colors <- c(
  "Donation"      = "#D55E00",
  "Certification" = "#E69F00",
  "Offsetting"    = "#0072B2"
)

all_exp |>
  mutate(
    ci_lo      = Coef - 1.96 * SE,
    ci_hi      = Coef + 1.96 * SE,
    Instrument = recode(Attr,
      don  = "Donation",
      cert = "Certification",
      off  = "Offsetting"
    ),
    Instrument = factor(Instrument, levels = c("Donation", "Certification", "Offsetting")),
    Dimension  = case_when(
      Label %in% c("Left (S/V/MP)", "Centre-right", "SD", "No pref / DK")
        ~ "A.  Party vote (q16)",
      Label %in% c("Low state trust", "Mid state trust", "High state trust")
        ~ "B.  State trust (q2_1)",
      TRUE
        ~ "C.  Policy consequentiality (q14)"
    ),
    Label = factor(Label, levels = subgroup_order)
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Coef, y = Label, color = Instrument, xmin = ci_lo, xmax = ci_hi)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_pointrange(position = position_dodge(width = 0.55),
                  linewidth = 0.5, size = 0.35) +
  scale_color_manual(values = instr_colors) +
  facet_wrap(~Dimension, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    x     = "Coefficient relative to tax baseline (95% CI)",
    y     = NULL,
    color = NULL,
    caption = "Tax is the omitted baseline (coefficient = 0). Exploratory analyses, not pre-registered."
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    legend.key.size  = unit(0.4, "cm"),
    strip.background = element_blank(),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0, size = 9),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.caption     = element_text(size = 7, hjust = 0, colour = "grey40"),
    axis.text.y      = element_text(size = 8)
  )
```
